metadata.title: Why wrapping one email in tags stops Claude from writing "Dear Claude"
metadata.topic: Marking where data ends and instructions begin
metadata.purpose: Claude was trained to treat XML tags as an organizing mechanism; wrapping variable input in tags marks its start and end explicitly, so the model stops guessing where the data ends.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro am_onyx) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.still_lanes: c2v (template vs substituted prompt, tags highlighted)
metadata.visual_object: A prompt template with a highlighted variable slot; on substitution the highlight dissolves into surrounding text, then XML tags snap back around it
metadata.hook: A prompt template is crystal-clear to a human, but once user input is substituted in, Claude can't tell your instruction from the data it's supposed to act on.

beats.narration_text: Template "Yo Claude, [EMAIL] — make this more polite." User's email happens to open "Yo Claude." After substitution Claude reads the whole thing as the email and begins its rewrite "Dear Claude." Wrap the email in `<email>...</email>` and the confusion disappears.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: The boundary between instruction and data is obvious to a human; after substitution Claude reads across it; why does an XML tag fix what plain text couldn't?
A prompt template is crystal-clear to a human, but once user input is substituted in, Claude can't tell your instruction from the data it's supposed to act on.
Claude was trained to treat XML tags as an organizing mechanism; wrapping variable input in tags marks its start and end explicitly, so the model stops guessing where the data ends.
beats.visual: A prompt template with a highlighted variable slot; on substitution the highlight dissolves into surrounding text, then XML tags snap back around it
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: `<email>Yo Claude, thx 4 the help</email>` → polished rewrite; without tags → "Dear Claude…".
Claude was trained to treat XML tags as an organizing mechanism; wrapping variable input in tags marks its start and end explicitly, so the model stops guessing where the data ends.
